name: Explore Codebase
desc: High-level map of an unfamiliar codebase
prompt: /goat-debug investigate this codebase. If I don't tell you where to focus, produce a high-level map in 300 words or less with sections: Entry points, Core domains, External integrations, Test hotspots. Read actual files - don't guess from names. Ask me what area to drill into next.

name: Diagnose Error
desc: Diagnosis only — stops before proposing a fix
prompt: /goat-debug diagnose an error. Ask me to paste the error message, stack trace, and what I was doing when it triggered. Stop before proposing a fix - I want to understand the cause first. Ask questions if the stack trace is ambiguous or the repro steps are unclear. End with 'Root cause: ...' or 'Cannot determine root cause from current evidence; need: ...'

name: Fix Bug
desc: Diagnosis through to fix and post-fix verification
prompt: /goat-debug fix a bug end-to-end. Ask me for expected vs actual behaviour and steps to reproduce. Don't expand scope to "while we're here" refactors — fix only what's necessary. Call out any regression risk for related code paths before declaring done.

name: Code Review
desc: Two-pass review of recent changes with MUST/SHOULD/MAY severity
prompt: /goat-review my recent changes on this branch. Flag findings as MUST (blocks merge), SHOULD (fix before it spreads), or MAY (nitpick — skip if time is tight). Ask questions if the scope is unclear.

name: Quality Audit
desc: Area audit of the most-changed files by commit frequency
prompt: /goat-review audit the most-changed files in this repo. Find hotspots by commit frequency, then review for code smells, complexity creep, test gaps, and unclear ownership. Prioritise findings by risk × change frequency — a small smell in a frequently-changed file beats a big one in a stable file.

name: Review Uncommitted
desc: Pre-commit gate — MUST findings only, no fix suggestions
prompt: /goat-review my uncommitted changes as a pre-commit gate. Flag MUST findings only — bugs, security issues, broken tests, missing null checks, regression risks. Skip SHOULD and MAY. If no MUST findings, say "ship it" and stop. Don't offer fixes — I want to decide.

name: Review Instructions
desc: Audit agent instruction files for drift and contradictions
prompt: /goat-review audit my agent instruction files (AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md) for drift. Check for: stale references, contradictions between files, missing verification gates, broken cross-references. Verify every claim by reading the actual file or running the command — do not assume it's correct because it's written down.

name: Critique GOAT Flow
desc: Qualitative read-only audit of the goat-flow installation (not the CLI audit command)
prompt: /goat-review audit the goat-flow installation in this project. This is the qualitative read-only assessment - not the CLI 'goat-flow audit' command which runs deterministic checks with scoring and JSON output. Read-only scope: AGENTS.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md, .goat-flow/config.yaml, .goat-flow/skill-reference/, installed skills under .claude/skills/, .agents/skills/, .github/skills/, plus hooks and active-agent config. Check for setup quality, contradictions, stale documentation, and duplicated content.

name: Plan Feature
desc: Feature brief to structured plan with scope boundaries
prompt: /goat-plan a new feature. Ask me for the feature brief (problem being solved, users, success criteria, constraints). Produce a plan with scope boundaries (in vs out), affected files/modules, spike/POC needs, assumption list, and risk callouts. Not a task list yet — that's the next step. Ask questions if the brief is ambiguous.

name: Break Into Milestones
desc: Milestone task files with testable exit criteria
prompt: /goat-plan break this into milestones. Use the feature plan from context; if none, ask which feature. Each milestone gets: goal (one sentence), exit criteria (testable), key tasks. M1 is always a spike validating the riskiest assumption. No milestone longer than 3-5 days of work — split if larger.

name: Plan Refactor
desc: Sequenced refactor plan with blast-radius analysis
prompt: /goat-plan a refactor. Ask me what to restructure and the expected blast radius. Map actual callers, tests, and dependencies — use grep, don't guess. Propose a sequenced plan that preserves behaviour at each step. Flag any API-breaking changes that require caller updates.

name: Critique a Plan
desc: SKEPTIC/ANALYST/STRATEGIST critique of a plan
prompt: /goat-critique this plan. Apply the SKEPTIC, ANALYST, and STRATEGIST lenses. Concrete findings, not vibes. Ask which plan to critique if none in context.

name: Critique an Artifact
desc: Multi-lens critique of an assessment, strategy, or review findings
prompt: /goat-critique an artifact. Ask me what to critique (assessment, strategy, review findings, test strategy, architecture proposal) if nothing is in context. Flag anything stated without evidence, any alternative not considered, and any dependency that could invalidate the whole thing.

name: Pressure-Test a Skill
desc: RED/GREEN/REFACTOR skill TDD using the quality-testing protocol
prompt: /goat-critique pressure-test a goat-flow skill using .goat-flow/skill-reference/skill-quality-testing.md. Ask me which skill. Apply the RED/GREEN/REFACTOR cycle: RED - construct an adversarial scenario that should trip the skill. GREEN - run the skill and record whether it handled the scenario. REFACTOR - propose one minimal change to the skill that would close the gap. Ask questions if the scenario scope is unclear.

name: Targeted UI Testing Plan
desc: Code-change risk analysis with targeted UI testing tasks
prompt:
/goat-qa Use gh to carefully read the GitHub issue link and all comments, then analyse the code changes in the pull request. Git checkout the branch and analyse the code changes locally to determine a highly targeted testing plan.

If I haven't provided the git branch, GitHub issue URL, or PR URL, ask me for them before proceeding.

First, identify what TYPE of change this PR makes, then target where bugs hide for that type (database: nullable columns NULL in production; form: excluded fields nullified on submit; API: entity-level auth gaps; cron: no user context + full dataset iteration; UI: data-dependent rendering; business logic: rounding/timezone/state transitions; cross-cutting: side effects across entry points).

Do NOT include what the developer has already tested, or what will be covered by the project's static analysis or existing automated acceptance tests.

Above the table: 'Excludes what dev + walk-through already covered, and anything static analysis / automated acceptance tests catch.'

Present as a markdown table with three columns: Test Task | Why Important | Result
- Test Task: concrete step-by-step with arrows (e.g. 'Pin 2+ notes -> change colour -> save -> reload. Pins should remain.')
- Why Important: code references encouraged (file paths, method names help triage)
- Result: leave empty for tester to fill

Hard excludes - do NOT create tasks for: DB seeding, multi-tab concurrency (unless code introduces concurrency primitives), address-bar URL manipulation, request tampering/extensions, CLI/cron/shell. UI testing only on local development. If a real risk falls outside these constraints, flag it in a note below the table.

Aim for 3-8 tasks of around 5-10 minutes each. Ask questions if required.

name: Coverage Audit
desc: Test coverage depth audit for a codebase area
prompt: /goat-qa audit test coverage in a codebase area. Ask which area - default to the most-changed files. Output as a table: File | Existing tests | Assertion depth | Uncovered branches | Risk. Don't conflate 'file has tests' with 'file is well-tested'.

name: Regression Guard
desc: Test capturing the invariant violated by a recent bug
prompt: /goat-qa regression guard for a recent bug fix. Ask me for the bug details (what broke, root cause, fix applied) if not in context. Write a test that captures the invariant that was violated - not just the happy path. Name the test so the bug is discoverable later: test_<module>_<invariant>_regression_<issue-id>. Place it near existing tests for the affected module.

name: User Flow Diagram
desc: Mermaid flow diagram traced from actual code paths
prompt: /goat-qa produce a Mermaid user-flow diagram for QA handoff. Ask which feature if not in context. Trace actual code paths (controllers, services, external calls) - don't guess based on feature name. Include entry points, decision branches, external calls, error paths, and success states. If a branch is inferred rather than code-traced, mark it INFERRED (dashed edge in Mermaid).

name: Generate Walk-Through Comment
desc: Two-part walk-through notes: plain-English summary and tester-voice discussion questions
prompt:
/goat-qa Use gh to carefully read the GitHub issue link and all comments, then analyse the code changes in the pull request. Git checkout the branch and read the code locally to understand what was actually built - not just what the PR description claims.

If I haven't provided the git branch, GitHub issue URL, or PR URL, ask me for them before proceeding.

Generate walk-through notes in two parts:

**Part 1 - Summary (2 lines, plain English)**
Two lines max. Plain language describing what changed and what behaviour a tester or user would notice. No file names, no code terms.

**Part 2 - Discussion Questions for Dev and Tester (3-5)**
Output 3-5 sharp questions designed to surface risks the developer may have missed. Skip anything the developer already tested or documented in their PR description/dev notes, anything static analysis would catch, and anything existing automated acceptance tests cover.

First, identify what TYPE of change this PR makes, then probe where bugs actually hide for that type:
- Database/migration: nullable columns NULL in production but populated in test data. Legacy records predating the migration.
- Form/validation: conditionally excluded fields silently nullified on submit. Multi-step abandonment leaving partial records.
- API/endpoint: entity-level authorization gaps. Inconsistent behaviour across entry points (web, API, background, webhooks).
- Cron/background: no user context. Full dataset iteration at production scale. Incomplete workflow states.
- UI/frontend: data-shape-dependent rendering - empty states, single vs multiple, long strings.
- Business logic: rounding/precision compounding. Timezone edge cases. Status transitions and boundary values.
- Cross-cutting: side effects not triggered for all entry points. Coexisting current vs deprecated paths.

Voice rules: NO file paths, line numbers, method names, class names in question text. Lead with what you found: 'From what I can see...' or 'I noticed that...' followed by 'Is that intended?' or 'Should we be worried about...'

Each question: bold one-line question in propose-and-ask voice, then 1-2 plain-English sentences of context. Use hyphens (-) not em-dashes.

Do NOT output: Verification Integrity footer, Change Risk Map, Gap Analysis, or TL;DR.

Self-check before outputting: re-read each question. If any file path, method name, class name, or line number appears in the question text, rewrite it in plain English.

Aim for fewer questions on small/low-risk PRs, more on complex ones. Ask clarifying questions before generating output if the scope is genuinely unclear.

name: Security Assessment
desc: Full threat assessment with CONFIRMED/PROBABLE/THEORETICAL findings
prompt: /goat-security full threat assessment of this project. Read the codebase, config, and deployment surface - not just package manifests. Classify findings as CONFIRMED (reproduced or clearly exploitable), PROBABLE (strong evidence, needs validation), or THEORETICAL (attack class exists but exploitation path unclear). For each finding, name the asset at risk, the attacker model, and the precondition. Check each defense-in-depth layer independently: authentication, feature toggles, entity-level authorization, attribute/role checks.

name: Compliance Gap Check
desc: Compliance-mode assessment mapped to a specific framework
prompt: /goat-security compliance-mode assessment. Ask me which framework (SOC 2, HIPAA, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, etc.). Map each applicable control to evidence in the codebase — quote the actual clause, show the actual implementation or absence. Don't fabricate compliance claims — if you can't find evidence, say "not assessed".

name: Dependency Scan
desc: CVE and supply-chain check on dependencies
prompt: /goat-security scan dependencies for known CVEs and supply-chain risks. Cross-reference the lockfile for exact versions. Flag any dependencies with no updates in 2+ years or from unmaintained sources.

name: Critique Security Findings
desc: Multi-lens critique challenging overstated or missing evidence
prompt: /goat-critique this security assessment using three lenses. SKEPTIC: which findings are overstated or lack evidence? What's the actual exploitability vs the claimed severity? ANALYST: which findings are missing evidence or rely on theoretical attack chains? What's the actual blast radius? STRATEGIST: what structural fixes would prevent whole classes of issues vs tactical one-offs? Don't rubber-stamp.

name: Mermaid Flow Diagram
desc: Trace code flow for a feature and produce a Mermaid flowchart
prompt: /goat-qa Trace the code flow for a specific feature or ticket and produce a Mermaid flowchart showing: entry point (controller/route/command), key decision branches (if/else, switch, feature flags), external calls (API, queue, email, cron), where errors are caught vs where they bubble up unhandled, and the final outcome(s). Keep the diagram readable - collapse internal helper methods unless they contain branching logic. Label edges with the condition that triggers them. If a branch is inferred rather than verified from the code, mark it INFERRED (dashed edge in Mermaid). Output as a ```mermaid code block. Ask which feature or ticket to trace if not in context.

name: Test Plan vs Code Changes
desc: Compare your testing plan against actual code changes to find gaps
prompt: /goat-qa Compare a testing plan against the actual code changes in a PR or branch. Ask me to paste my testing plan and dev testing notes if not provided. Compare this combined testing coverage against the actual diff. Surface: gaps (code changes neither the dev nor my plan covers), overlaps (areas where my plan duplicates what the dev already tested), suggestions (specific scenarios to add based on actual code), and risk areas (changes that look low-risk but touch shared logic or multiple entry points). Be specific - 'test the edge cases' is not useful. 'Test with a user who has no email address because the notification code does not null-check the email field' is useful. Ask for the PR link or branch name if not provided.

name: Automated Coverage Check
desc: Identify what static analysis and automated tests already cover before manual testing
prompt: /goat-qa Before manual testing, identify what automated tooling already covers for this PR so testers don't duplicate machine coverage. Check: (1) your project's static analyser - which of these code changes would it catch if broken? List specific examples from this PR. (2) Existing acceptance tests - are there tests covering any of the flows changed? List what they assert, noting shallow assertions (page-loads-only vs verifying content). (3) Unit tests - are there tests covering the changed logic? What do they assert? Label each finding as VERIFIED (you read the test/config) or INFERRED (based on patterns). Then tell me: given what's already automated, where should manual testing focus? What can only a human tester catch here? Ask for the PR link or branch name if not provided.
